item.title: Re: Does Samsara actually *have* 16 members?
item.content:encoded: Aha, understood now, thanks!

item.title: Re: Does Samsara actually *have* 16 members?
item.content:encoded: Samsara has four core members (Irantu, Onru, Munru, Nanku) and then twelve supporting regular human operatives. One of the creators, sirpudding, assembled a handy org chart.

item.title: Does Samsara actually *have* 16 members?
item.content:encoded: The text describes 16 members of MTF Tau-5 Samsara, but there are four in all other places I saw. Did they just clone them an extra three times just for this mission?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I should admit upfront that this could just be my religious bias. But… this kinda just feels like an excuse to shoot up God. There's very little reason given for the Foundation to attack in the first place The angels react lethally to anyone who approaches… but they're not doing anything, as far as I can tell, that would require the Foundation to come that close anyway. If the goal is to contain them, it's not clear why a 10 km no-go zone couldn't do the job. There's also no indication of what God and the angels are doing here- they don't do anything at all except run into bullets and summon drizzly weather. They're less active and/or threatening than a guy with a shotgun hollering at kids to get off his lawn. This not just makes the assault (seemingly) unnecessary, but removes most of the bad @$$-ness as well.

Even the ending doesn't add much; the retrieved writings don't add much overall, except a bit of confusion (are we dealing with the Abrahamic God, the Mesopotamian pantheon, or both?) and the possibility of the events impacting the agents is brought up, then more or less ignored with a snappy line from the assault leader.

Tl,dr: The article does a good job of describing the assault, but everything around it falls flat, and as a result it feels like the Foundation just decided to bully God and brag about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At long last an article that presents Task Forces as badasses instead of bumbling buffoons or oblivious ninnies that get their asses handed to them and are completely overwhelmed by any situation. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Samsara and Hammer Down in a joint operation? I knew this was gonna be good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's an online first draft1 pair of books called The Salvation War by Stuart Slade that deals with a very similar premise, and goes into detail about how it may play out.

Long story short, Hell (and Heaven) get their asses kicked six ways to sunday by humanity. It seems that mighty angels and terrible demons aren't a match for cruse missiles, tanks, jet fighters, and nukes.

Footnotes
1. Story is, it got leaked/pirated before it was finished, and gained a perception of being a money sink afterwards by publishing firms. The planned third book was never completed because of this. Piracy is bad, mmm'kay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could be wrong, but I took it as not so much that God lost power, as that humans became more powerful and left God in the dust. I mean, that angelic army sounds like it would have been practically undefeatable before the invention of firearms and explosives. With modern weapons, vehicles, and other technology, though, humanity definitely has the advantage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept looking for a reason they would authorize an expedition into its interior knowing full well the hostile intent of the guards and relatively self contained nature of the SCP already. It's a really well written fight scene and the line from the interview is fantastic, but I can't see the Foundation willing to potentially throw lives at something unless it obviously poses a clear threat or a potentially huge gain. The beginning of this article does neither of those things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not my usual cup of tea (not that… my usual "cup of tea" for scps skews this direction in the first place, but y'know) but I like this look at the Tower of Babel. I already knew this was about the Tower of Babel before from hearing you talk about it in chat, but I didn't read any of the earlier drafts so I had no idea that this was the direction you were taking it; I'd actually assumed it would be more about the tower itself and possibly human folly of believing in the ability to reach God.

I was going to ask if there's any reason within the context of this SCP for God's apparent loss of power, but I guess it's not really necessary to know. For whatever reason, God's gone from knocking down the Tower of Babel in one fell swoop to not even being able to keep the ruins from being invaded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, an article about Samsara giving god the finger can really brighten up your day. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The action is poor, the interview feels like an excuse to drop that singular line, the final section feels bloated, and the whole thing is reliant on shock value.

As a side note, this article reminds me a bit of The Salvation War, not that I liked TSW either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well damn, this sure changed a lot in the two days since I read it.

Considering my two big issues were the randomness of them using a border interment facility and why they had to process people on earth, I’m a huge fan of the changes to the fight’s context. The new final logs are also fantastic, and the implication at the end was just amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the science vs gods of it. That being said, I was expecting something that go deeper into the theme after the event log, like an (tragic) account of events from the angelic side. Perhaps due to that expectation, I found the item log disconnecting. Even though the last item touches on the theme again, it wasn't a full exploration of it. Nevertheless, you have my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, all I want from an article is some clever humour, a good story arc and some badass fight scenes, and this delivers on all accounts. The dialogue is tight, the action engaging, and I could read the whole thing quickly and still feel satisfied — a major plus in my book. You handled an initially absurd concept very well, and it didn't seem as forced as some other articles with similar themes. I'm also becoming increasingly aware of Samsara's "badass MTF" status, which is something I wholeheartedly support: the site needs more explosions, goddammit.

There's almost nothing here that even hints at a downvote for me, you play off all the elements so well. I don't have a huge amount more to say that hasn't been said by Shaggy or Nanaya above me, except a congratulations for writing possibly my favourite Abrahamic scip on the site. Well done, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article for a huge amount of reasons, but… it feels like there should be more to this whole article. I, for one, would like to see a set of exploration logs detailing the insides of this city and possibly an accidental Foundation raid on Heaven. Then again, I'm a lunatic, so… +1 anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the exact same reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If god exists, it can't shoot worth a damn and I've got close air support.

Upvoted just for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final line of the interview perfectly encapsulates the thrust of a good portion of this article - it's unabashedly badass. The event log was colorful and action-packed, suffering not a moment of inane exposition, and was incredibly satisfying as far as these types of things go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ruins_of_the_Chaldean_Catholic_cathedral_on_the_citadel_in_Kirkuk,_disputed_between_Kurdistan_Region_and_Iraq_01.jpg

Some texts on this page were adapted from real life babylonian works found here: http://oracc.museum.upenn.edu/cams/selbi/corpus

The works themselves are obviously public domain and the translations are CC 3.0 SA unported.

Also, thanks to Hippo and ARD for helping me refine the idea quite a bit.

+ show block
– hide block

Mama put my guns in the ground
I can't shoot them anymore
That cold black cloud is comin' down
Feels like I'm knockin' on heaven's door
